summary: Charity Majors argues that the operations profession is bifurcating: infrastructure expertise is moving to cloud providers while a new discipline called platform engineering is emerging to help software engineers self-serve and own their code in production. Platform engineering teams function as internal product teams that bridge third-party services and developer needs, representing the natural evolution of DevOps in a cloud-native world.
key_points: Operations is splitting into deep infrastructure (at IaaS companies) and broad operability (platform engineering at product companies)
Platform engineering teams operate like product teams with PMs, UX researchers, and a focus on developer experience rather than infrastructure management
The key principle of platform engineering: make it easy to do the right things and hard to do the wrong things
Strong platform teams need a mix of operations experience and software development skills, not just software engineers building developer tools
DevOps is becoming irrelevant as companies go cloud-native and no longer need to manage multiple generations of infrastructure
money_quotes: Developer cycles are the scarcest resource in your company, and you want to spend as many of those as possible on your core product: the crown jewel, the code that makes you a business.
One of the key principles of any developer platform is that it should be easy to do the right things, and hard to do the wrong things.
A strong platform team has an equally deep grounding in operations experience and software development.
Disrespecting operational expertise is the shortest path to never again sleeping through the night.
It is also on its way to becoming irrelevant. We are swiftly entering a post-DevOps world.
key_insight: Platform engineering is the natural successor to DevOps, shifting the focus from managing infrastructure to enabling developers to self-serve and own their code in production through curated, product-like internal platforms.
